subject: PHYSICS
unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line

distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 3.4 Acceleration, 3.5 Uniform Motion, 3.6 Graphical Representation of Motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A car is located at \(x = 5.0\,\mathrm{m}\) on a straight road, with the origin at the start line. What is the car's position?

options.A: The distance the car has travelled from the start line
options.B: The car's location relative to the origin, which is \(5.0\,\mathrm{m}\)
options.C: The total length of the path from the start line
options.D: The speed of the car at that instant

questions.hint: Position is a measured location relative to a reference point.
questions.explanation: Position is defined as a measurement of a location with respect to a reference point, such as the origin. Option A describes distance, which is the total path length, not the instantaneous location.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Position is a measurement of a location, with respect to some reference point (usually an origin).

explanations_tiered.definition: Position is the location of an object relative to a reference point, typically the origin of a coordinate system.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Position describes where an object is located along a path or in space. It is measured from a chosen reference frame, and its value can be positive or negative depending on direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a number line. Position is like the exact mark on that line where you are standing, using zero as your starting point.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Position is the measured location of an object with respect to an origin. For straight-line motion, it is often given as a coordinate on the x-axis.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Position is a vector quantity in 3D space, but in one-dimensional motion it is represented by a scalar coordinate \(x\). The change in position, \(\Delta x = x_f - x_i\), defines displacement.
reasoning_steps: Identify the reference point (origin).
Locate the object's coordinate relative to that origin.
Express the coordinate as the position.
misconceptions: Position is the same as distance traveled. Incorrect because distance is the total path length, while position is a specific location.
Position can never be negative. Incorrect; positions left of the origin are assigned negative values.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Position: location relative to origin. One-dimensional: \(x\).

glossary_entry.definition: The location of an object relative to a reference point, often the origin of a coordinate system.

flashcard.back: Position is the location of an object with respect to a reference point (origin). For 1D motion, it is given as a coordinate like \(x = 5\,\mathrm{m}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What defines the position of an object on a straight line?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Its coordinate relative to a chosen origin, e.g., \(x = 5\,\mathrm{m}\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The location of an object relative to a reference point, usually the origin.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse position with distance. Emphasize that position is a single point, distance is a path length.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define position relative to a reference frame
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Position is the location relative to an origin, not the distance traveled.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A runner completes a 400-meter lap around a track and stops at the starting point. Which quantity measures the total path length the runner covered?
questions.hint: Think of the total ground covered regardless of direction.
questions.explanation: Distance is defined as the total path length traveled by a body during its motion, which in this case is 400 m. Displacement would be zero because the runner returned to the start.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Distance travelled is a measure of the actual distance covered during the motion of a body.

explanations_tiered.definition: Distance is the total length of the actual path traveled by an object during its motion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Distance measures how much ground an object has covered, regardless of direction. It is a scalar quantity with only magnitude.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine walking around your house back to the start. Distance is every step you took added together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Distance is the total path length traveled. It does not depend on the starting or ending point, only on the route taken.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Distance \(S\) is a scalar that sums the magnitudes of all infinitesimal path segments \(dS\). It is always non-negative and path-dependent.
reasoning_steps: Identify the entire path the object moved along.
Add the lengths of all segments of the path.
The sum is the distance.
misconceptions: Distance can be negative. Incorrect; distance is always positive because it is a scalar representing path length.
Distance is the same as displacement. Incorrect; displacement is the straight-line change in position, while distance is the actual path.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Distance: total path length. Scalar, always positive.

glossary_entry.definition: The total length of the path traveled by an object.

flashcard.back: Distance is the total path length traveled. Example: a 400-m lap covers 400 m of distance.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does distance differ from displacement?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Distance is the total path length (scalar); displacement is straight-line change in position (vector).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The total path length traveled by an object.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that distance is path-dependent, while displacement is not.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between position, distance and displacement
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Distance measures the total path length covered (400 m). Displacement would be 0 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: If an object moves from \(x = 10\,\mathrm{m}\) to \(x = 4\,\mathrm{m}\) along a straight line, what is its displacement?
questions.hint: Displacement is final position minus initial position.
questions.explanation: Displacement \(\Delta x = x_f - x_i = 4\,\mathrm{m} - 10\,\mathrm{m} = -6\,\mathrm{m}\). The negative sign indicates direction opposite the positive axis. Option C gives the magnitude only, ignoring direction.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Displacement is the change in the student's position, \( \vec{S}_f - \vec{S}_i\)

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the change in position of an object, given by final position minus initial position.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Displacement is a vector quantity that describes the straight-line change in position from start to finish, including direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk from the 10 m mark to the 4 m mark, your displacement is how far you are from the start, with a sign for direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Displacement \(\Delta \vec{S} = \vec{S}_f - \vec{S}_i\) indicates both magnitude and direction. Here it is \(-6\,\mathrm{m}\), meaning 6 m left.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement is the integral of velocity over time: \(\Delta \vec{S} = \int \vec{v} dt\). For constant velocity, \(\Delta \vec{S} = \vec{v} \Delta t\).
worked_solution: Step 3: Apply formula \(\Delta x = x_f - x_i = 4 - 10 = -6\,\mathrm{m}\).
reasoning_steps: Subtract the initial position from the final position.
The result is the displacement, including sign for direction.
misconceptions: Displacement is always positive. Incorrect; displacement includes direction and can be negative.
Displacement equals distance. Incorrect; displacement is the straight-line change, distance is total path.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Displacement: \(\Delta x = x_f - x_i\). Vector, can be negative.

glossary_entry.definition: The change in position of an object, including direction.

flashcard.back: Displacement = final position − initial position. Example: from 10 m to 4 m gives \(-6\,\mathrm{m}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the displacement from 10 m to 4 m?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that the sign of displacement indicates direction relative to the chosen axis.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate displacement from initial and final positions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(\Delta x = 4 - 10 = -6\,\mathrm{m}\). Option C is the magnitude only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A car travels \(120\,\mathrm{km}\) in \(2.0\,\mathrm{hours}\). What is its average speed in \(\mathrm{m/s}\)?
questions.hint: Use \(v_{av} = \frac{\text{total distance}}{\text{total time}}\). Convert km to m and hours to seconds.
questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time = \(120\,\mathrm{km} / 2.0\,\mathrm{h} = 60\,\mathrm{km/h}\). Converting: \(60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 16.7\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Option B is the speed in km/h without conversion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Average speed = Total distance covered / Total time taken

explanations_tiered.definition: Average speed is the total distance traveled divided by the total time taken.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Average speed gives the overall rate of motion over a journey, ignoring variations in instantaneous speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drive 120 km in 2 hours, on average you cover 60 km each hour. That's your average speed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Average speed = total distance / total time. Here \(v_{av} = 120/2 = 60\,\mathrm{km/h}\). Convert to m/s: \(60 \times 1000/3600 = 16.7\,\mathrm{m/s}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Average speed is a scalar calculated as \(v_{av} = \frac{1}{t_{tot}} \int |\vec{v}| dt\). For constant speed, it equals that speed.
reasoning_steps: Divide total distance by total time.
Convert units if necessary.
misconceptions: Average speed is the same as average velocity. Incorrect; velocity includes direction, speed does not.
Average speed is just the average of instantaneous speeds. Not generally true unless time intervals are equal.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Average speed = total distance / total time. Scalar.

glossary_entry.definition: Total distance traveled divided by total time.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate average speed?
flashcard.back: \(v_{av} = \frac{\text{total distance}}{\text{total time}}\). Example: 120 km in 2 h gives \(60\,\mathrm{km/h}\) or \(16.7\,\mathrm{m/s}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A car travels 120 km in 2.0 h. What is its average speed in m/s?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize unit conversions, a common source of error.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute average speed in m/s from km/h
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(v_{av} = 120/2 = 60\,\mathrm{km/h} = 16.7\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Option B is the speed in km/h.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What does a car's speedometer directly measure?
questions.hint: The speedometer shows the speed at a particular moment.
questions.explanation: A speedometer gives the speed of the car at a specific instant in time, which is the instantaneous speed. Average speed is total distance over total time, not shown by the speedometer.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: The speedometer of a car gives information about the instantaneous speed of the car.

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous speed is the speed of an object at a specific instant in time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity, shown by devices like a speedometer.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The speedometer needle moves as you drive; the number you see right now is your instantaneous speed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Instantaneous speed is the rate of change of distance at a particular moment, given by the slope of the distance-time graph at that point.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous speed is defined as \(v = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta t} = \frac{dS}{dt}\), the derivative of distance with respect to time.
reasoning_steps: Consider a very short time interval around the instant of interest.
Compute the limit of distance over time as the interval approaches zero.
misconceptions: Instantaneous speed is the same as average speed over the whole trip. Incorrect; average speed is total distance/total time.
The speedometer measures average speed. Incorrect; it measures real-time speed.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Instantaneous speed: speed at a specific moment.

glossary_entry.definition: The speed of an object at a particular instant.

flashcard.front: What does a car's speedometer measure?
flashcard.back: It measures instantaneous speed, the speed at that exact moment.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Instantaneous speed, the speed at a specific moment.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with average speed using a trip odometer and clock.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between average speed and instantaneous speed
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The speedometer shows instantaneous speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
questions.question: What physical quantity is defined as the rate of change of displacement?
questions.hint: It is a vector involving displacement over time.
questions.explanation: Velocity is defined as the rate of change of displacement with respect to time. Speed is the rate of change of distance, not displacement.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
source_trace.content_anchor: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.

explanations_tiered.definition: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with respect to time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Velocity is a vector quantity that includes both how fast an object moves and the direction of motion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Velocity tells you not just how fast, but also which way you are going (e.g., 60 km/h north).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Velocity is defined as displacement divided by time, \(\vec{v} = \Delta \vec{S} / \Delta t\). It is a vector.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous velocity is the derivative \(\vec{v} = d\vec{S}/dt\). The magnitude of velocity is speed.
reasoning_steps: Recall that velocity involves displacement (change in position) and time.
Understand that direction matters.
misconceptions: Velocity is the same as speed. Incorrect; velocity includes direction.
Velocity can be negative only if the object is slowing down. Incorrect; negative velocity indicates opposite direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of change of displacement; a vector.

flashcard.back: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, a vector quantity (e.g., 20 m/s east).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Velocity = rate of change of displacement. \(\vec{v} = \Delta \vec{S} / \Delta t\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rate of change of displacement, vector.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define velocity as rate of change of displacement
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Velocity is the rate of change of displacement; speed is for distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
questions.question: A person walks \(10\,\mathrm{m}\) east, then \(4\,\mathrm{m}\) west in a total time of \(6\,\mathrm{s}\). What is the average velocity?
questions.hint: Average velocity = total displacement / total time. Displacement is final position minus initial position.
questions.explanation: Total displacement = \(10\,\mathrm{m}\) east + \(4\,\mathrm{m}\) west = \(6\,\mathrm{m}\) east. Average velocity = \(6\,\mathrm{m} / 6\,\mathrm{s} = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east. Option A incorrectly uses total distance (\(14\,\mathrm{m}/6\,\mathrm{s}\)).

calculation_steps: Displacement = 10 m east + 4 m west = 6 m east

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
source_trace.content_anchor: Average velocity of a body is the total displacement covered by that body in a specified direction divided by the total time

explanations_tiered.definition: Average velocity is total displacement divided by total time taken.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Average velocity considers the net change in position (displacement) and direction, not the total path length.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 10 m east then 4 m west, you end up 6 m east from start. That displacement over time is your average velocity.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Average velocity \(\vec{v}_{av} = \Delta \vec{S} / \Delta t\). Here \(\Delta \vec{S} = 6\,\mathrm{m}\) east, \(\Delta t = 6\,\mathrm{s}\), so \(\vec{v}_{av} = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Average velocity is the constant velocity that would produce the same displacement over the same time interval.
worked_solution: Step 2: Divide by total time: \(6\,\mathrm{m} / 6\,\mathrm{s} = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east.
reasoning_steps: Find net displacement (final position − initial position).
Divide by total time.
Include direction.
misconceptions: Average velocity is always positive. Incorrect; it can be negative if net displacement is opposite the positive direction.
Average velocity uses total distance. Incorrect; it uses displacement.

glossary_entry.definition: Total displacement divided by total time.

flashcard.front: A person walks 10 m east, 4 m west in 6 s. Average velocity?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is average velocity different from average speed?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Average velocity uses displacement (vector); average speed uses distance (scalar).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with Example 3.2 to show distance vs displacement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute average velocity from displacement and time
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Displacement = 6 m east; \(v_{av} = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east. Option A uses total distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
questions.question: A car's velocity changes from \(10\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east to \(20\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east in \(5\,\mathrm{s}\). What happens to the acceleration if the car instead changes from \(10\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east to \(0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east in the same time?

options.A: Acceleration is the same magnitude but negative
options.C: Acceleration is larger in magnitude and positive
options.D: Acceleration is smaller in magnitude but positive

questions.hint: Acceleration depends on change in velocity. Compare \(\Delta v\) for both cases.
questions.explanation: Acceleration \(\vec{a} = \Delta \vec{v} / \Delta t\). First case \(\Delta v = +10\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Second case \(\Delta v = -10\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Magnitude is same, sign opposite. Option A correctly identifies same magnitude but negative.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
source_trace.content_anchor: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Acceleration is a vector that indicates how quickly velocity changes, either in magnitude or direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Acceleration is how quickly you speed up or slow down. Speeding up gives positive acceleration; slowing down gives negative.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\vec{a} = \Delta \vec{v} / \Delta t\). The magnitude is the same in both cases (\(2\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\)), but the direction (sign) is opposite because the change in velocity is opposite.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Acceleration can be positive (speeding up in positive direction) or negative (slowing down). Deceleration is negative acceleration relative to velocity direction.
reasoning_steps: Calculate change in velocity (\(\Delta v = v_f - v_i\)).
Divide by time interval.
Compare magnitudes and signs.
misconceptions: Negative acceleration always means slowing down. Incorrect; if velocity is negative, negative acceleration can mean speeding up in the negative direction.
Acceleration is only about speed. Incorrect; changing direction also causes acceleration.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Acceleration = change in velocity / time. Vector.

flashcard.back: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. \(\vec{a} = \Delta \vec{v} / \Delta t\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A car goes from 10 m/s east to 20 m/s east in 5 s, then from 10 m/s east to 0 m/s east in 5 s. Compare accelerations.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Same magnitude (\(2\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\)), opposite direction (positive vs negative).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that direction of acceleration depends on change in velocity, not just speeding up/slowing down.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain acceleration as rate of change of velocity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Magnitude = 2 m/s² in both cases, but sign opposite.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.
questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of uniform motion?
questions.explanation: Uniform motion is motion with constant velocity, meaning both speed and direction do not change. Constant acceleration would mean velocity changes uniformly, not constant velocity.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.
source_trace.content_anchor: Uniform motion is the motion of an object along a straight line with a constant velocity or speed in a given direction.

explanations_tiered.definition: Uniform motion is motion with constant velocity (constant speed and direction).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In uniform motion, the object covers equal distances in equal time intervals and moves in a straight line.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Uniform motion means you're moving in a straight line at a steady pace, never speeding up, slowing down, or turning.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Uniform motion requires constant velocity: speed is constant and direction does not change. Acceleration is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Uniform motion implies \(\vec{v} = \text{constant}\), so \(\vec{a} = 0\). Position varies linearly with time: \(\vec{S}(t) = \vec{S}_0 + \vec{v}t\).
reasoning_steps: Check if speed is constant.
Check if direction is constant.
If both are constant, motion is uniform.
misconceptions: Uniform motion means constant speed but can turn. Incorrect; direction must also be constant.
Uniform motion is the same as uniform acceleration. Incorrect; uniform acceleration means velocity changes uniformly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Uniform motion: constant velocity, zero acceleration.

glossary_entry.definition: Motion with constant velocity along a straight line.

flashcard.back: Uniform motion is motion with constant velocity (constant speed and straight-line direction).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Constant velocity (constant speed and straight-line direction).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with non-uniform motion using a car accelerating or braking.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Uniform motion is constant velocity; constant acceleration is different.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: On a velocity-time graph for a car moving in a straight line, the slope of the line is constant and positive. What does this indicate about the car's motion?
questions.hint: The slope of a v-t graph is acceleration.
questions.explanation: The slope of a velocity-time graph gives the acceleration. A constant positive slope means constant positive acceleration. Constant velocity would give zero slope. Option A would be a horizontal line.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: The slope of a velocity-time graph is the acceleration of the object.

explanations_tiered.definition: The slope of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration of the object.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On a v-t graph, a straight line means constant acceleration; the slope (rise/run) gives the magnitude and sign of acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the v-t graph is a straight line going upward, the car is speeding up steadily (constant acceleration).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Slope of v-t graph = \(\Delta v / \Delta t = a\). Constant positive slope means constant positive acceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant acceleration, \(v(t) = v_0 + at\), which is a linear function. The slope of this line is \(a\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the vertical axis as velocity, horizontal as time.
Calculate slope = rise/run = change in velocity / change in time.
Recognize that slope equals acceleration.
misconceptions: The slope of a v-t graph is speed. Incorrect; it's acceleration.
A positive slope always means the object is moving forward. Incorrect; velocity could be negative but increasing (less negative).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: v-t graph slope = acceleration. Constant slope = constant a.

glossary_entry.definition: A graph showing how velocity changes over time; slope gives acceleration.

flashcard.front: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
flashcard.back: The slope of a v-t graph gives the acceleration. Constant slope = constant acceleration.

visual_system.visual_title: Velocity vs Time for Constant Acceleration
visual_system.visual_description: A straight line with positive slope on a velocity vs time graph, indicating velocity increases uniformly with time.
visual_system.caption: A constant positive slope on a v-t graph means constant positive acceleration.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Line graph with time on x-axis and velocity on y-axis. A straight line starts at (0,5) and goes up to (10,25), showing a constant positive slope.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: On a velocity-time graph, what does a straight line with positive slope mean?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It means constant positive acceleration; velocity increases uniformly with time.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Graph of velocity vs time; slope = acceleration.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: v-t slope = acceleration. Straight line = constant a.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the graph to show that slope = Δv/Δt = a.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Interpret velocity-time graphs to determine acceleration
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The slope of a v-t graph is acceleration; constant positive slope = constant positive acceleration.
